ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general 'tea beverage' class (FOODON_03301099). No specific class exists for yuzu honey black tea; closest_parent used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 AN0AN covers tea and tea-based beverages. No specific code exists for yuzu honey black tea; closest_parent used.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: SNOMED CT 16313001 represents tea as a substance. No specific concept for yuzu honey black tea; closest_parent used.

preparation_method: served cold or at room temperature

serving.natural_language_portion: One large cup of yuzu honey black tea, approximately 500 ml, as typically served at KOI Thé and similar Taiwanese bubble tea chains in Singapore.

nutrient_source.title: Sweetened black tea beverage, ready-to-drink — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy / Foundation Foods; supplemented with honey composition data (USDA FDC #169640) and yuzu juice reference (FAO/INFOODS SEA food composition analogy)
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact USDA or HPB entry exists for KOI Thé yuzu honey black tea. Values are estimated by combining: (1) brewed black tea base (~1 kcal/100 ml, negligible macros per USDA FDC #171917), (2) honey contribution (~30 g honey per 500 ml serving, USDA FDC #169640: 304 kcal/100 g, 82.4 g sugar/100 g), and (3) yuzu juice contribution (~10 ml per 500 ml serving, negligible macro impact). Blended per-100 ml values are derived from this composition model. The large (L) size at KOI Thé is approximately 500 ml. Sugar content reflects a standard sweetness level (50% sugar reduction assumed as default; full sugar would be higher). Values represent a mid-range estimate.

gi_source.title: Honey, GI = 55 (Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008. Diabetes Care. 2008;31(12):2281-2283); black tea base GI negligible; blended GI estimated at 55 reflecting honey as primary glycemic contributor.
gi_source.organization: Diabetes Care — American Diabetes Association peer-reviewed journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for KOI Thé yuzu honey black tea. GI is estimated at 55 based on honey GI = 55 (Atkinson et al. 2008, Diabetes Care), which is the primary glycemic contributor. Black tea base has negligible GI. Yuzu juice contributes minimal carbohydrate. Glycemic load per 500 ml serving = (GI × carbohydrate_g_per_serving) / 100 = (55 × 35.0) / 100 = 19.25, rounded to 19.3.

hypertension.key_factor: Low sodium content; no added salt
hypertension.guidance: Yuzu honey black tea contains approximately 15 mg sodium per 500 ml serving, posing negligible sodium load for individuals managing hypertension, though total daily sugar intake should still be monitored.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Added honey and sugar syrup contribute significant free sugars and a moderate glycemic load
type2_diabetes.guidance: A large serving of yuzu honey black tea contributes approximately 35 g of free sugars and a glycemic load of ~19, which can cause meaningful postprandial blood glucose elevation; individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should request reduced or zero sugar and limit portion frequency.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Negligible saturated fat and cholesterol; moderate free sugar intake warrants attention
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Yuzu honey black tea contains no saturated fat or cholesterol, and black tea polyphenols may offer modest cardioprotective benefit, but regular consumption at full sweetness adds free sugars that can adversely affect triglyceride levels in individuals with cardiovascular disease.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Yuzu Honey Black Tea (Large) is a sweetened cold tea beverage sold at KOI Thé, a Taiwanese bubble tea chain with outlets across Singapore including Funan Mall. The drink is made from brewed black tea blended with yuzu citrus flavouring or juice and honey syrup, served in a large cup of approximately 500 ml. Yuzu (Citrus junos) is a tart East Asian citrus fruit with a distinctive floral-citrus aroma that is widely used in Japanese and Taiwanese beverage culture. The honey adds natural sweetness and a mild floral note, complementing the astringency of the black tea base. The beverage is dairy-free, gluten-free, and halal-suitable. Nutritionally, a large serving provides approximately 140 kcal, predominantly from free sugars contributed by honey and any added sugar syrup. The fat content is negligible and protein is trace. The glycemic index is estimated at 55 (low) based on the honey component, and the glycemic load per large serving is approximately 19, which is clinically significant for individuals managing blood glucose. Black tea polyphenols, including theaflavins and thearubigins, may offer antioxidant and mild cardioprotective effects. Individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should request reduced sugar. The drink is a popular afternoon beverage in Singapore's Taiwanese tea café culture and is commonly ordered as a refreshing citrus-forward alternative to milk tea.

confidence_reason: No exact nutritional entry for KOI Thé yuzu honey black tea exists in any approved primary source (HPB Singapore Foodo, USDA FDC, FAO/INFOODS SEA). Values are estimated from component analysis: brewed black tea base (USDA FDC #171917), honey (USDA FDC #169640), and yuzu juice analogy. The serving size of 500 ml for the large cup is a reasonable estimate based on standard Taiwanese tea chain large cup conventions. GI is estimated from honey GI per Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care). Confidence is therefore medium.
notes: 1. fat_g is 0.0: naturally zero, as this is a tea-and-honey beverage with no fat-containing ingredients. Confirmed consistent with USDA FDC brewed black tea (#171917) and honey (#169640) composition data. 2. saturated_fat_g, monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, cholesterol_mg are 0.0: naturally zero by ingredient composition. 3. dietary_fibre_g is 0.0: naturally zero for a liquid tea beverage with no particulate fibre-containing ingredients. 4. vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg are -1: not reported in the component sources used for this estimation; values are below reliable detection threshold for a dilute tea beverage. 5. glycemic_index set to 55 (low) based on honey GI from Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care), the primary glycemic contributor; no direct GI measurement exists for this product. 6. Serving size of 500 ml (500 g equivalent) is an estimate for the KOI Thé large cup; actual volume may vary slightly by outlet. 7. Sugar content assumes 50% sugar level (KOI Thé default); full sugar would yield approximately 10–12 g sugar per 100 ml. 8. This record is cuisine-tagged Taiwanese following the input source; multilingual naming follows the Singaporean dish rules as the food is served in Singapore and the cuisine is Taiwanese (a non-Singaporean cuisine), so Chinese, Malay, Tamil, and Hindi names are provided to support Singapore multilingual retrieval context.
